Civic Records Pipeline — Technical Specification

A specification for the first deployable version of the civic-records project's pipeline layer. Designed to be implementable from this document alone, by Claude Code or any sufficiently competent engineer.

Status: Specification v1.0.0 — ready for implementation. License: CC0 1.0 Universal (public domain dedication). Companion documents: SCHEMA.md (v0.3.0+), IGA_TEMPLATE.md, sources.yml.

Section 1 — Goals and non-goals

Goals

Build the smallest pipeline that satisfies the following requirements:

  1. Persist records that conform to the v0.3.0 schema in a queryable datastore.
  2. Maintain an append-only, cryptographically-chained audit log of every modification.
  3. Ingest candidate records from external fetchers (the existing legistar.py tool and future siblings) via a documented HTTP API.
  4. Serve records and the audit log through a read API consumable by browsers, CLI tools, or other backend services.
  5. Deploy to Cloudflare's free tier without modification.
  6. Be portable to any Linux host running Python and SQLite without modification.
  7. Operate fully open-source under CC0 with no vendor-specific code paths in the core logic.

Non-goals

The following are deliberately out of scope for v1 and should not shape design decisions:

  1. Web UI beyond a minimal read view for verification purposes.
  2. Authentication and authorization beyond a single shared API key for ingestion. Multi-tenant access control is a future feature.
  3. Automatic cross-referencing using NLP, entity resolution, or fuzzy matching. Cross-references are created manually or by exact external-ID match.
  4. CORA workflow automation. The schema supports CORARequest records but the pipeline doesn't drive the CORA process.
  5. Real-time streaming, websockets, push notifications. The pipeline is request-response only.
  6. Performance optimization beyond what's necessary for the expected scale (~10,000 records, ~100,000 audit log entries, ~10 ingestions per day).

Explicit design principles

These are not implementation details; they are commitments that shape every other decision.

  1. Portability over cleverness. Every feature must work locally on a developer laptop with Python and SQLite, and on Cloudflare with Workers and D1. Where these conflict, dual implementations are acceptable; vendor-specific cleverness in the core logic is not.

  2. Schema fidelity. The pipeline is an implementation of the v0.3.0 schema. When the spec and the schema document diverge, the schema document wins, and the pipeline is updated.

  3. Provenance everywhere. Every record, every document, every field originating outside the system carries provenance. This is a non-negotiable invariant enforced at the application layer (and, where possible, at the database layer).

  4. Append-only audit log. The signed_actions table is append-only. The system MUST NOT support deletion or modification of audit log entries. Compaction (per schema Article 6.4) is a future feature requiring unanimous consent and is not implemented in v1.

  5. Automation proposes, humans dispose. Records ingested from automated fetchers are marked verified_by_human: false. Promotion to verified status requires an explicit signed action by an authorized human actor.

Section 2 — Architecture

2.1 High-level structure

The system is composed of four loosely-coupled components:

   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   Fetchers (Py)     │   Run anywhere (laptop, server, Worker)
   │   - legistar.py     │   Output: JSON candidate records
   │   - socrata.py      │
   │   - pprbd.py        │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ HTTP POST (candidate records)
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   Ingestion API     │   Validates, persists, signs
   │   (Worker / WSGI)   │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  Datastore (D1 or   │   Records, documents, audit log
   │  SQLite file)       │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   Read API          │   GET records, search, audit log
   │   (Worker / WSGI)   │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  Public Read View   │   Static HTML + fetch()
   │  (Pages / file://)  │
   └─────────────────────┘

2.2 Dual deployment targets

The pipeline MUST support two deployment modes, sharing code where possible.

Mode A — Local Python. The pipeline runs as a Python application on any system with Python 3.10+ and SQLite. The datastore is a single SQLite file. The ingestion and read APIs are served by a WSGI or ASGI server (the spec recommends flask or starlette, but http.server from the standard library is acceptable for v1).

Mode B — Cloudflare. The pipeline runs on Cloudflare's developer platform. The datastore is D1 (SQLite-compatible). The APIs are Workers. The static read view is hosted on Pages. Deployment is via wrangler.

The codebase is organized so that 80%+ of code is shared between the two modes. The differences are isolated in adapter modules:

pipeline/
  core/                  # 100% shared logic
    schemas.py           # Pydantic models matching v0.3.0
    validation.py        # Schema validation
    audit.py             # Signed action construction and chain hashing
    ingestion.py         # The actual ingestion logic
    queries.py           # Read query construction
  adapters/
    db_sqlite.py         # SQLite (local mode)
    db_d1.py             # D1 (Cloudflare mode)
    http_local.py        # Local HTTP serving (Flask or stdlib)
    http_worker.py       # Cloudflare Worker entrypoint
  fetchers/              # Adapters for source-specific tools
    legistar_adapter.py  # Maps legistar.py output to ingestion API
  cli/                   # Local CLI tools
    ingest.py            # Manual ingestion from JSON files
    export.py            # Dump records to JSON
    link.py              # Manually create cross-references
    log.py               # Query the audit log

The core module never imports adapter modules. Adapter modules import core. This is the dependency rule.

2.3 Why this works for both targets

Cloudflare's D1 is SQLite-compatible. The same CREATE TABLE statements work in both. The same SQL queries work in both, with caveats:

  • D1 doesn't support PRAGMA statements that affect connection state (foreign keys are always on, journal mode is fixed).
  • D1 doesn't support ATTACH DATABASE.
  • D1's free tier has size limits (5 GB total, 100k writes/day) that local SQLite doesn't.

The adapter modules abstract these differences. The core code uses a narrow database interface (execute, executemany, fetchone, fetchall, transaction support) that both adapters implement.

For HTTP, both Flask/Starlette and Cloudflare Workers accept HTTP requests and return HTTP responses. The core handler logic takes a parsed request object and returns a response object; the adapters translate between framework-specific objects and the shared types.

Section 3 — Data model

3.1 Tables

The schema's v0.3.0 entities map to the following D1/SQLite tables. Field types are chosen for portability — no D1-specific or SQLite-specific types are used beyond TEXT, INTEGER, REAL, and BLOB.

-- Jurisdictions: governments participating in the platform.
-- Maps to schema's JurisdictionalAuthority entity.
CREATE TABLE jurisdictions (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,                 -- e.g., "juris_us_co_colorado_springs"
    human_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,       -- e.g., "us-co-colorado-springs"
    display_name TEXT NOT NULL,
    type TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- enum from schema § JurisdictionalAuthority.type
    parent_jurisdiction_ids TEXT,        -- JSON array of jurisdiction IDs
    participation_status TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'active_publisher' | 'signed_consumer' | 'referenced_only' | 'withdrawn'
    iga_references TEXT,                 -- JSON array of IGA record IDs
    primary_record_types TEXT,           -- JSON array
    records_custodian TEXT,              -- JSON object
    publishing_endpoints TEXT,           -- JSON array
    external_data_sources TEXT,          -- JSON array
    joined_at TEXT,                      -- ISO 8601 date
    withdrew_at TEXT,                    -- ISO 8601 date
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,            -- ISO 8601 datetime UTC
    updated_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL               -- Full JSON representation
);

CREATE INDEX idx_jurisdictions_status ON jurisdictions(participation_status);
CREATE INDEX idx_jurisdictions_type ON jurisdictions(type);

-- Records: the primary entity.
CREATE TABLE records (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    schema_version TEXT NOT NULL,
    type TEXT NOT NULL,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    summary TEXT,
    status TEXT NOT NULL,
    governing_jurisdiction_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    referenced_jurisdiction_ids TEXT,    -- JSON array
    external_ids TEXT,                   -- JSON array of {system, value, verified, source}
    parties TEXT,                        -- JSON array
    financial TEXT,                      -- JSON object
    timeline TEXT,                       -- JSON object
    geography TEXT,                      -- JSON object
    tags TEXT,                           -- JSON array of strings
    manifest_completeness TEXT,          -- JSON object computed from documents
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL,              -- Canonical JSON
    FOREIGN KEY (governing_jurisdiction_id) REFERENCES jurisdictions(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_records_jurisdiction ON records(governing_jurisdiction_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_records_type ON records(type);
CREATE INDEX idx_records_status ON records(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_records_updated ON records(updated_at);

-- Documents: manifest entries on records.
CREATE TABLE documents (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    document_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    status TEXT NOT NULL,                -- 'available' | 'pending' | 'inferred' | 'denied' | 'action_needed'
    expected_to_exist INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,  -- boolean
    expectation_basis TEXT,
    file_url TEXT,                       -- For 'available' status
    file_sha256 TEXT,
    file_size_bytes INTEGER,
    file_mime_type TEXT,
    expected_release_path TEXT,          -- e.g., 'cora_to_procurement_services'
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (record_id) REFERENCES records(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_documents_record ON documents(record_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_documents_status ON documents(status);

-- External IDs: extracted for fast lookup. Duplicated from records.external_ids.
-- This is a denormalization for performance; raw_json is canonical.
CREATE TABLE record_external_ids (
    record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    system TEXT NOT NULL,
    value TEXT NOT NULL,
    verified INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- boolean
    PRIMARY KEY (record_id, system, value),
    FOREIGN KEY (record_id) REFERENCES records(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_external_ids_lookup ON record_external_ids(system, value);

-- Provenance: per-record provenance entries.
CREATE TABLE provenance (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    record_id TEXT,
    document_id TEXT,
    source_type TEXT NOT NULL,           -- 'api' | 'scrape' | 'cora_response' | 'human_authored' | etc.
    source TEXT,
    fetched_at TEXT,
    verified_by_human INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    notes TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (record_id) REFERENCES records(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (document_id) REFERENCES documents(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_provenance_record ON provenance(record_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_provenance_document ON provenance(document_id);

-- Cross-references: typed relationships between records.
CREATE TABLE cross_references (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    source_record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    target_record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    relation TEXT NOT NULL,
    note TEXT,
    asserted_by TEXT NOT NULL,           -- 'human' | 'automated_rule' | 'external_api'
    promotion_status TEXT,               -- For mention-promoted refs
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (source_record_id) REFERENCES records(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (target_record_id) REFERENCES records(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_xref_source ON cross_references(source_record_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_xref_target ON cross_references(target_record_id);

-- Mentions: textual references not yet promoted to cross-references.
CREATE TABLE mentions (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    source_record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    target_record_id TEXT,
    target_external_reference TEXT,
    relation TEXT NOT NULL,
    context TEXT,
    promotion_status TEXT NOT NULL,      -- 'not_promoted' | 'candidate_for_promotion' | 'promoted'
    asserted_by TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (source_record_id) REFERENCES records(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (target_record_id) REFERENCES records(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_mentions_source ON mentions(source_record_id);

-- Signed actions: the audit log. APPEND-ONLY.
CREATE TABLE signed_actions (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    timestamp TEXT NOT NULL,
    actor_type TEXT NOT NULL,            -- 'human' | 'system' | 'fetcher' | 'automation'
    actor_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    actor_display_name TEXT,
    actor_role TEXT,
    actor_jurisdiction_authorities TEXT, -- JSON array
    action_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    target_type TEXT NOT NULL,           -- 'record' | 'document' | 'cora_request' | etc.
    target_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    before_state TEXT,                   -- JSON; null for creates
    after_state TEXT,                    -- JSON; null for deletes (which we don't do, but reserved)
    reason TEXT,
    chain_prev_hash TEXT,                -- SHA-256 of previous action (chained)
    chain_self_hash TEXT NOT NULL,       -- SHA-256 of this action's content + prev_hash
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL
);

CREATE INDEX idx_actions_target ON signed_actions(target_type, target_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_actions_actor ON signed_actions(actor_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_actions_timestamp ON signed_actions(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_actions_type ON signed_actions(action_type);

-- CORA requests: open public records requests.
CREATE TABLE cora_requests (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    record_id TEXT,                      -- The record this request is about
    agency TEXT NOT NULL,
    department_id TEXT,
    submitted_via TEXT,
    external_request_id TEXT,
    submitted_at TEXT,
    submitted_by TEXT,                   -- JSON object
    request_text TEXT,
    fee_estimate INTEGER,                -- USD cents
    fee_paid INTEGER,
    status TEXT NOT NULL,
    raw_json TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (record_id) REFERENCES records(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_cora_record ON cora_requests(record_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_cora_status ON cora_requests(status);

-- Schema migrations: track applied DDL changes.
CREATE TABLE schema_migrations (
    version TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    applied_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    description TEXT
);

3.2 Data integrity rules

Enforced at the application layer (not all enforceable in SQL alone):

  1. Append-only audit log. The application MUST NOT issue UPDATE or DELETE against signed_actions. The deployment MAY further enforce this with database permissions, but this is not required in v1.

  2. Chain integrity. Every signed_action's chain_self_hash MUST equal SHA-256(canonical_json(action_payload) || chain_prev_hash). On ingestion, the previous action's chain_self_hash becomes the new action's chain_prev_hash. The first action ever has chain_prev_hash = NULL.

  3. Provenance required. Every record and every document MUST have at least one provenance row.

  4. Foreign key integrity. All foreign keys are enforced. D1 has foreign keys on by default; SQLite needs PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON on every connection.

  5. JSON canonicalization. When computing hashes for the audit log, JSON MUST be canonicalized: keys sorted alphabetically, no extraneous whitespace, UTF-8 encoded. The implementation should use a single canonicalization function imported throughout.

3.3 Schema migrations

Migrations are SQL files in migrations/, numbered sequentially (001_initial_schema.sql, 002_add_xyz.sql). The application checks the schema_migrations table on startup and applies any pending migrations in order. Each migration is idempotent: it either succeeds fully or fails without partial application (wrap in a transaction).

For v1, only 001_initial_schema.sql exists, containing the DDL above.

Section 4 — APIs

4.1 Ingestion API

Endpoint: POST /v1/ingest

Authentication: Bearer token in Authorization header. The token is a single shared secret stored in environment variable INGEST_API_KEY. Multi-tenant authentication is out of scope for v1.

Request body: JSON, one of:

{
  "operation": "create_record",
  "actor": { ... },
  "record": { ... }
}

{
  "operation": "update_record",
  "actor": { ... },
  "record_id": "rec_...",
  "patch": { ... },
  "reason": "..."
}

{
  "operation": "ingest_candidate",
  "actor": { ... },
  "candidate": { ... }
}

For ingest_candidate, the system attempts to match the candidate to an existing record by external IDs. If a match is found, this becomes an update_record with a computed patch. If no match is found, this becomes a create_record.

Response 200:

{
  "operation": "create_record",
  "record_id": "rec_...",
  "matched_existing": false,
  "signed_action_id": "act_...",
  "manifest_completeness": { ... }
}

Response 400: Validation error. Body contains errors array with field-level details.

Response 401: Missing or invalid auth token.

Response 409: Conflict (e.g., create with duplicate id).

Response 422: Schema validation error.

Response 500: Internal error. The audit log is unaffected; no partial writes.

4.2 Read API

Endpoint: GET /v1/records/:id

Returns the record, its documents, its cross-references, and a summary of its signed actions. Public — no auth required for v1.

{
  "record": { ... },
  "documents": [ ... ],
  "cross_references": [ ... ],
  "mentions": [ ... ],
  "signed_actions_summary": {
    "count": 12,
    "first": "2026-05-09T...",
    "last": "2026-05-15T...",
    "actors": ["fetcher_legistar", "human_emma"]
  }
}

Endpoint: GET /v1/records/:id/signed-actions

Returns the full audit log for a record. Paginated with ?limit=N&before=ID.

Endpoint: GET /v1/records?type=contract&jurisdiction=us-co-colorado-springs

Search records by type, jurisdiction, status, date range, or tag. Query params:

  • type — record type
  • jurisdiction — jurisdiction human_id
  • status — record status
  • tag — tag (multiple tag params allowed; OR semantics)
  • updated_since — ISO 8601 datetime
  • limit — max 100, default 20
  • cursor — opaque pagination cursor

Endpoint: GET /v1/jurisdictions

List jurisdictions with their participation status.

Endpoint: GET /v1/audit-log

System-wide audit log. Same pagination as record-level log. Public.

Endpoint: GET /healthz

Liveness check. Returns {"status": "ok", "schema_version": "0.3.0"}.

4.3 Public read view

A static HTML site under /public/ (or pages/ for Cloudflare Pages). About 6 pages:

  1. index.html — Project overview, link to record search, link to audit log.
  2. record.html — Renders one record, fetched by ID from query param.
  3. search.html — Search form, fetches from /v1/records? with filters.
  4. audit-log.html — Renders the system-wide audit log.
  5. jurisdictions.html — Lists jurisdictions.
  6. about.html — Links to schema, IGA template, source code.

No JavaScript framework. Vanilla fetch(). About 800 lines of HTML, CSS, and JS total. CSS is functional (readable typography, sane spacing) but not designed; this is a verification surface, not a public-facing product.

Section 5 — Component specifications

5.1 core/schemas.py

Pydantic v2 models for every schema entity. One model per entity: Record, Document, Provenance, CrossReference, Mention, SignedAction, Jurisdiction, CORARequest, Actor.

Each model:

  • Has all fields from the schema with correct types and required-ness.
  • Includes a model_config with extra = "forbid" to catch typos in candidate records.
  • Has a to_canonical_dict() method that returns the model as a dict with sorted keys, suitable for hashing.
  • Has a to_db_row() method that returns a dict matching the table schema.

5.2 core/audit.py

Functions for constructing and verifying signed actions.

def construct_signed_action(
    actor: Actor,
    action_type: str,
    target_type: str,
    target_id: str,
    before_state: dict | None,
    after_state: dict | None,
    reason: str,
    chain_prev_hash: str | None,
) -> SignedAction:
    """Build a SignedAction with proper chain hash."""

def verify_chain(actions: list[SignedAction]) -> bool:
    """Verify the chain hash sequence over a list of actions in order."""

def canonical_json(obj: dict) -> str:
    """Canonical JSON: sorted keys, no extraneous whitespace, UTF-8."""

def hash_action(payload: dict, prev_hash: str | None) -> str:
    """SHA-256 hash of canonical_json(payload) || (prev_hash or "")"""

5.3 core/validation.py

Schema validation logic. Validates candidate records before ingestion.

def validate_candidate(candidate: dict) -> ValidationResult:
    """
    Validate a candidate record against schema rules.

    Checks:
    - Required fields present
    - Field types correct
    - governing_jurisdiction_id refers to a known jurisdiction (or
      auto-creates a referenced_only jurisdiction)
    - At least one provenance entry
    - external_ids well-formed
    - status in allowed enum
    - type in allowed enum
    """

@dataclass
class ValidationResult:
    valid: bool
    errors: list[ValidationError]
    warnings: list[str]

5.4 core/ingestion.py

The primary ingestion logic. Pure functions; takes a database interface as a parameter rather than importing one directly.

def ingest_candidate(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    candidate: dict,
    actor: Actor,
) -> IngestionResult:
    """
    Ingest a candidate record.

    1. Validate against schema.
    2. Look up existing record by external IDs.
    3. If found: compute patch, apply update, log signed action.
    4. If not found: create new record, log signed action.
    5. Recompute manifest_completeness if documents changed.
    6. Return result with action ID and matched-existing flag.

    All operations within a single transaction.
    """

def manual_create_record(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    record: dict,
    actor: Actor,
) -> IngestionResult: ...

def manual_update_record(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    record_id: str,
    patch: dict,
    actor: Actor,
    reason: str,
) -> IngestionResult: ...

def create_cross_reference(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    source_id: str,
    target_id: str,
    relation: str,
    actor: Actor,
    note: str | None = None,
) -> IngestionResult: ...

5.5 core/queries.py

Read-side query construction. Returns query specifications, not actually executes them. Adapter modules execute against the appropriate database.

@dataclass
class RecordQuery:
    type: str | None = None
    jurisdiction_human_id: str | None = None
    status: str | None = None
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    updated_since: datetime | None = None
    limit: int = 20
    cursor: str | None = None

def get_record_with_relations(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    record_id: str,
) -> dict:
    """Fetch a record plus its documents, cross-references, mentions."""

def search_records(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    query: RecordQuery,
) -> SearchResult: ...

def get_audit_log(
    db: DatabaseInterface,
    record_id: str | None = None,
    limit: int = 100,
    cursor: str | None = None,
) -> AuditLogResult: ...

5.6 adapters/db_sqlite.py

SQLite implementation of DatabaseInterface. Uses sqlite3 from the standard library. Opens the database file from a configurable path (default civic-records.db). Enables foreign keys on every connection.

5.7 adapters/db_d1.py

D1 implementation of DatabaseInterface. Uses Cloudflare's D1 binding from within a Worker. Translates the same execute, fetchone, fetchall interface to D1's prepare/bind/run/all API.

5.8 adapters/http_local.py

Local HTTP server. Recommended: Flask. Routes for the ingestion API endpoints and the read API endpoints. Reads the API key from environment variable INGEST_API_KEY. Serves static files from pages/ for the public read view.

5.9 adapters/http_worker.py

Cloudflare Worker entrypoint. Same routes, implemented as a Worker fetch handler. Uses env.DB for D1 binding, env.INGEST_API_KEY for auth.

5.10 fetchers/legistar_adapter.py

Adapter that takes the existing legistar.py tool's output and posts it to the ingestion API. Two modes:

  • File mode: reads a JSON file produced by legistar.py, posts candidates one at a time.
  • Live mode: invokes legistar.py programmatically, captures its output, posts candidates.

5.11 CLI tools

Standalone Python scripts in cli/:

  • cli/ingest.py [file.json] — Read a JSON file of candidates and send them to the ingestion API.
  • cli/export.py --record-id ID — Fetch a record and write to stdout or file.
  • cli/link.py --from ID --to ID --relation REL — Manually create a cross-reference.
  • cli/log.py [--record-id ID] [--since DATE] — Print audit log entries.
  • cli/migrate.py — Apply pending schema migrations.
  • cli/seed.py — Insert canonical jurisdictions for first-time setup.

All CLI tools read configuration from environment variables:

  • CIVIC_RECORDS_API_URL — base URL of the ingestion API
  • CIVIC_RECORDS_API_KEY — auth token
  • CIVIC_RECORDS_DB_PATH — path to SQLite file (local mode only)

Section 6 — Testing

6.1 Test categories

Three layers, in order of increasing scope:

Unit tests. Test pure functions in core/. No database, no network. Use pytest. Target ~200 tests covering:

  • Schema validation edge cases
  • Audit chain construction and verification
  • Canonical JSON behavior across edge cases (Unicode, nested arrays, null values, etc.)
  • Patch computation
  • Query construction

Integration tests. Test against an in-memory SQLite database. Exercise the full ingestion pipeline with synthetic candidates. Target ~50 tests covering:

  • Create then read a record
  • Update via candidate matching
  • Cross-reference creation
  • Audit log retrieval
  • Foreign key integrity
  • Append-only enforcement

Smoke tests. Test against a real running instance. Run before deployment and on a schedule against the deployed instance. About 10 tests covering:

  • Health check responds
  • Ingestion API accepts a known-good candidate
  • Read API returns the ingested record
  • Audit log shows the ingestion event
  • Error paths return correct status codes

6.2 Test data

tests/fixtures/ contains JSON files with realistic candidate records. Reuse the worked examples from the project (Axon contract, Lorson Ranch permit, IGA record) as the seed set. These fixtures also serve as documentation of what good candidates look like.

6.3 Fixtures for both deployment modes

The integration test suite runs twice in CI: once against in-memory SQLite, once against a local D1 emulator (Wrangler's --local mode). Same tests, different adapter. Failures in only one suite indicate adapter bugs.

Section 7 — Configuration

7.1 Environment variables

Variable Required Default Description
INGEST_API_KEY Yes none Bearer token for ingestion endpoints
CIVIC_RECORDS_DB_PATH Local only civic-records.db Path to SQLite file
CIVIC_RECORDS_LOG_LEVEL No INFO Python logging level
CIVIC_RECORDS_PORT Local only 8080 HTTP server port
CIVIC_RECORDS_HOST Local only 127.0.0.1 HTTP server host

For Cloudflare deployment, the same variables are set through wrangler.toml or the Cloudflare dashboard. The DB path is unnecessary because D1 is bound by name.

7.2 wrangler.toml

name = "civic-records"
main = "adapters/http_worker.py"
compatibility_date = "2026-01-01"

[[d1_databases]]
binding = "DB"
database_name = "civic-records"
database_id = "set-after-creation"

[[r2_buckets]]
binding = "DOCUMENTS"
bucket_name = "civic-records-documents"

[vars]
CIVIC_RECORDS_LOG_LEVEL = "INFO"

# INGEST_API_KEY is set as a secret via `wrangler secret put`

7.3 Local development

# First-time setup
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
python -m cli.migrate
python -m cli.seed

# Run the local HTTP server
export INGEST_API_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32)
python -m adapters.http_local

# Test ingestion
python -m cli.ingest tests/fixtures/axon-candidate.json

# Read a record
python -m cli.export --record-id rec_axon_c12102

# View audit log
python -m cli.log --record-id rec_axon_c12102

7.4 Cloudflare deployment

# First-time setup
wrangler login
wrangler d1 create civic-records  # capture database_id, paste into wrangler.toml
wrangler r2 bucket create civic-records-documents
wrangler d1 execute civic-records --file=migrations/001_initial_schema.sql
wrangler secret put INGEST_API_KEY  # paste a generated secret

# Deploy
wrangler deploy

# Verify
curl https://civic-records.your-account.workers.dev/healthz

Section 8 — Security

8.1 Threat model

The system handles public records, so confidentiality of record contents is generally not a concern. The threats that matter:

  1. Unauthorized writes. An attacker submits false records or modifies existing ones. Mitigated by the INGEST_API_KEY shared secret.
  2. Audit log tampering. An attacker modifies historical signed actions. Mitigated by the chain hash. Detection is possible via verify_chain.
  3. Denial of service. An attacker floods the ingestion endpoint. Cloudflare provides rate limiting; local mode is vulnerable but exposed only on localhost by default.
  4. PII leakage. Records may include personal information from public sources. Out of scope for v1; the project's policy on PII handling is a separate document.

8.2 Required practices

  • The INGEST_API_KEY MUST be at least 32 random bytes, base64 or hex encoded.
  • Logs MUST NOT include the full request body of ingestion calls (to avoid logging the auth header).
  • The read API MUST NOT expose any field marked as PII in the schema (none in v1, but the convention is established now).
  • HTTPS is required in production. Cloudflare provides this by default. Local development on localhost is exempt.

8.3 Out of scope for v1

  • Per-jurisdiction access control (Phase 3, requires IGA).
  • Per-actor authentication beyond the shared secret.
  • Encryption at rest beyond what the storage provider offers (D1 encrypts at rest; SQLite files do not).
  • Audit log signing with cryptographic signatures from individual actors (chain hash is sufficient for v1).

Section 9 — Observability

9.1 Logging

Structured logging using Python's logging module with JSON output. Every log entry includes:

  • timestamp (ISO 8601 UTC)
  • level (INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
  • event (short snake_case identifier)
  • record_id (when relevant)
  • actor_id (when relevant)
  • request_id (per-HTTP-request UUID)

Log events that MUST be emitted:

  • request_received — every HTTP request
  • request_completed — every HTTP response
  • ingestion_started
  • ingestion_completed
  • validation_failed
  • chain_verification_failed (CRITICAL)
  • migration_applied

9.2 Metrics

Metrics are nice-to-have for v1 but not required. If implemented, expose at /metrics in Prometheus exposition format:

  • Ingestion rate (requests/second)
  • Ingestion error rate
  • Records by jurisdiction
  • Audit log size
  • Read API latency

9.3 Health checks

GET /healthz responds 200 with {"status": "ok"} when:

  • Database connection succeeds
  • Latest migration is applied
  • A trivial query runs in under 100ms

Otherwise, 503 with details.

Section 10 — Documentation deliverables

The implementer MUST produce, alongside the code:

  1. README.md — Project overview, install, deploy, contribute.
  2. docs/architecture.md — Diagrams and explanation of the adapter pattern.
  3. docs/api.md — Full API reference with request/response examples.
  4. docs/operations.md — Runbook for common operational tasks (apply migration, view logs, debug a stuck ingestion).
  5. docs/contributing.md — How to add a new fetcher, how to add a schema field, how to run tests.
  6. CHANGELOG.md — Versioning and change history.
  7. Inline docstrings on every public function in core/ and adapters/.

Section 11 — Acceptance criteria

The pipeline is complete when ALL of the following hold:

  1. The codebase builds without errors on Python 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12.
  2. All unit tests pass.
  3. All integration tests pass against in-memory SQLite.
  4. All integration tests pass against Wrangler local D1.
  5. Smoke tests pass against a deployed Cloudflare instance.
  6. Smoke tests pass against a local Python deployment.
  7. The Axon C-12102 worked example record can be ingested as a candidate and read back through the read API, identical in content to the schema-mocked version.
  8. A multi-step verification scenario can be exercised end-to-end: ingest the Axon record, ingest a hypothetical Council resolution from a Legistar fetch, manually create a cross-reference between them, view the cross-reference through the read API, and see four signed actions in the audit log (one per ingestion, one for the cross-reference, one for the manifest_completeness recomputation).
  9. The audit chain can be verified end-to-end across all signed actions in a fresh database.
  10. README.md gives a complete onboarding path from "git clone" to "I have a running instance" in under 30 minutes for a competent developer.

Section 12 — Open questions for the implementer

These are decisions the spec deliberately leaves to the implementer's judgment. The implementer SHOULD record their choice in the codebase README or architecture doc.

  1. HTTP framework choice for local mode. Flask, Starlette, FastAPI, or stdlib? Recommendation: Flask for simplicity. FastAPI if automatic OpenAPI generation is wanted.

  2. Pydantic version. v1 or v2? Recommendation: v2. v1 is deprecated.

  3. JSON schema generation for the API. Use Pydantic's built-in schema export, or hand-write OpenAPI? Recommendation: Pydantic export, served at /openapi.json.

  4. Test runner. pytest, unittest? Recommendation: pytest.

  5. Code formatter. Black, ruff format, autopep8? Recommendation: ruff format with the project's pyproject.toml settings.

  6. Linter. ruff, flake8, pylint? Recommendation: ruff.

  7. Type checker. mypy, pyright? Recommendation: mypy in strict mode for core/, lenient elsewhere.

  8. Worker runtime version. Cloudflare Workers Python is in beta; pin to a specific compatibility date for stability.

  9. D1 vs SQLite differences. Document any cases where the same query behaves differently across the two adapters. Add notes to docs/architecture.md.

  10. Document storage. v1 uses R2 on Cloudflare and the local filesystem (./documents/) on local mode. Both are abstracted behind adapters/blob_storage.py. Implementation is permitted but deferred to v2.

Section 13 — Future work (informational, not part of v1)

The following are explicitly out of scope but worth knowing the implementer is designing toward:

  • Per-jurisdiction access control via the Actor.jurisdiction_authorities mechanism in the schema.
  • Automated cross-referencing using entity resolution.
  • CORA workflow tooling (drafting, submission, response tracking).
  • Citizen contribution review queue.
  • Watch mode for fetchers (notify on new matters).
  • Cross-jurisdictional federation queries spanning multiple platform instances.
  • Document storage and retrieval (PDFs, attachments) via R2.
  • Full-text search over record content and document text.
  • Public-facing UI suitable for non-technical users.

Section 14 — Implementation hints

A few non-binding suggestions that may save time:

Start with the unit tests. Write the schema validation tests before any code. The tests double as a specification of behavior and catch many design problems before they're embedded.

Build local mode first, then add the Cloudflare adapter. Local mode is faster to iterate on. Once it's working end-to-end, port to Cloudflare. The shared core/ should require no changes.

Use the existing worked examples as fixtures. The Axon contract, the Lorson Ranch permit, and the IGA record are all hand-assembled JSON in the project's existing artifacts. Drop them into tests/fixtures/ and use them throughout the test suite. They're also useful sanity checks: if your code can ingest them, the schema implementation is probably correct.

Resist scope creep. Every feature on the "future work" list is tempting. Resist. v1 is small, and small is the point. A working, deployed, minimal v1 is more valuable than a comprehensive v2 that isn't done yet.

Don't optimize prematurely. SQLite handles tens of millions of rows on laptop hardware. D1 has its own limits but they're far above v1 needs. Write straightforward code; profile only if a real performance problem surfaces.

Section 15 — Repository structure

The expected repository layout after implementation:

civic-records-pipeline/
├── README.md
├── LICENSE                          # CC0
├── CHANGELOG.md
├── pyproject.toml
├── requirements.txt
├── requirements-dev.txt
├── .gitignore
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── test.yml                # Run unit + integration tests
│       └── deploy.yml              # Deploy to Cloudflare on tag
├── wrangler.toml
├── migrations/
│   └── 001_initial_schema.sql
├── pipeline/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── schemas.py
│   │   ├── validation.py
│   │   ├── audit.py
│   │   ├── ingestion.py
│   │   └── queries.py
│   ├── adapters/
│   │   ├── db_sqlite.py
│   │   ├── db_d1.py
│   │   ├── http_local.py
│   │   └── http_worker.py
│   ├── fetchers/
│   │   └── legistar_adapter.py
│   └── cli/
│       ├── ingest.py
│       ├── export.py
│       ├── link.py
│       ├── log.py
│       ├── migrate.py
│       └── seed.py
├── pages/                          # Static read view
│   ├── index.html
│   ├── record.html
│   ├── search.html
│   ├── audit-log.html
│   ├── jurisdictions.html
│   └── about.html
├── tests/
│   ├── fixtures/
│   │   ├── axon-candidate.json
│   │   ├── lorson-ranch-permit.json
│   │   └── iga-record.json
│   ├── unit/
│   ├── integration/
│   └── smoke/
└── docs/
    ├── architecture.md
    ├── api.md
    ├── operations.md
    └── contributing.md

Appendix A — Glossary

  • Candidate record: A record submitted to the ingestion API whose existence and content have not yet been verified by a human.
  • Authoritative record: A record that has been verified by a human actor with appropriate jurisdictional authority.
  • Signed action: An entry in the audit log capturing one modification, with actor identity, before/after state, and a chain hash.
  • Manifest: The set of documents associated with a record.
  • Manifest completeness: A counter capturing how many of the expected documents on a record are actually available.
  • Provenance: The chain of sources establishing where a record or field came from.
  • Cross-reference: A typed relationship between two records.
  • Mention: A textual reference from one record to another that has not yet been promoted to a cross-reference.
  • Promotion: The act of upgrading a mention to a cross-reference, or a candidate record to an authoritative record.

Appendix B — References

  • SCHEMA.md v0.3.0 — the data model this pipeline implements.
  • IGA_TEMPLATE.md — the legal framework for multi-jurisdictional participation.
  • sources.yml — the catalog of public surfaces this pipeline ingests from.
  • legistar.py — the existing fetcher tool, the first integration.
  • The Cloudflare Workers documentation at developers.cloudflare.com.
  • The D1 documentation at developers.cloudflare.com/d1.
  • SQLite documentation at sqlite.org/docs.html.

End of specification.
